text: The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt using curved monopolar scissors, reflecting the bowel through a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon repositions the bowel to its original location, revealing the avascular plane developed between the posterior mesocolon and Gerota's fascia.
The fourth robotic arm grasps the anterior Girotis fascia and retracts the kidney anteriorly, aiding in the further mobilization of the bowel.
The surgeon delicately dissects the brighter yellow mesenteric fat from the paler fat of the Girotis fascia, revealing the distinct color contrast between these tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a robotic grasping instrument to spread open the space between the mesentery and Girota's fascia, facilitating incision of the tissue while safeguarding underlying structures.
The surgeon exposes the upper pole of the kidney by mobilizing the adjacent spleen or liver, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the kidney.
The surgeon releases the splenic flexure, pancreas, and splenic attachments, enhancing the exposure of the renal hilum for further dissection.
The surgeon uses scissors opened like a hook to carefully manipulate and separate tissue, minimizing trauma to underlying structures during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon releases the splenic attachments, enabling the spleen to be reflected medially, enhancing exposure of the renal hilum.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the spleen to avoid injury to the stomach, which is positioned behind the spleen and vulnerable during the procedure.
The assistant uses a long tip suction device to provide counter traction, aiding in the release of the upper pole attachments of the kidney.

The surgeon performs passive liver retraction for a right-sided tumor using a locking grasper inserted through a five millimeter subxiphoid port. The grasper is positioned under the liver and secured to the diaphragm.
